author: Athanasius of Alexandria
interpretation: Athanasius reads 'I and the Father are one' as a definitive assertion of the Son's unity of essence (homoousios) with the Father. The plural verb 'are' preserves personal distinction; the neuter 'one' (hen, not heis) indicates unity of substance, not identity of person. Arius's claim that the Son was a created being collapses against this verse — a creature cannot be 'one' with the Creator in this absolute sense.
context: This verse was a primary weapon in the Arian controversy. Athanasius's grammatical argument — neuter hen (one thing/substance) vs. masculine heis (one person) — became standard Trinitarian exegesis and informed the Nicene Creed's language.

author: Augustine of Hippo
source_title: Tractates on the Gospel of John
interpretation: Augustine draws from this verse the twin truths that guard against both Arianism and Sabellianism. 'Are' refutes Sabellius — the Father and Son are not the same person, since two subjects take a plural verb. 'One' refutes Arius — they share the same divine nature. Augustine holds both truths simultaneously: distinction of persons, unity of substance.
context: Augustine's anti-heretical double reading became the classic formulation of Latin Trinitarian theology. His De Trinitate works out the full implications of what 'one substance, three persons' means philosophically.

author: Thomas Aquinas
source_title: Commentary on the Gospel of John
interpretation: Aquinas systematizes the patristic reading: 'one' refers to the unity of essence, power, and operation shared by Father and Son. Whatever the Father does, the Son does equally, because they share a single divine nature. Aquinas further argues that this unity grounds the Son's ability to give eternal life (the immediate context in John 10) — only one who shares God's nature can confer what belongs to God alone.
context: Aquinas's integration of John 10:30 with its narrative context — the sheep whom no one can snatch — elevated the verse from a proof text for Trinitarian doctrine to a statement about the security of believers held by divine power.

author: John Calvin
source_title: Commentary on the Gospel According to John
interpretation: Calvin reads the statement in its pastoral context first: Jesus assures believers that they are doubly secured — held by both the Father and the Son, who are one in power and will. Calvin then affirms the doctrinal import: the statement necessarily implies shared divine essence, since no creature could claim to be 'one' with God in the sense of sharing his sovereign power over all things.
context: Calvin's pastoral-first, doctrinal-second approach exemplifies his exegetical method. He resists using verses as mere doctrinal proof texts divorced from their narrative and rhetorical context.

author: Karl Barth
interpretation: Barth reads John 10:30 as the ground of all Christian knowledge of God. We do not know the Father apart from the Son — the unity Jesus claims is not merely metaphysical but epistemological. To see Jesus is to see the Father, to hear Jesus is to hear the Father. Any theology that bypasses Christ to reach God directly contradicts the very unity Jesus declares here.
context: Barth's Christocentric reading of John 10:30 reflects his broader rejection of natural theology. The verse does not merely affirm ontological unity but establishes Christ as the exclusive point of access to divine knowledge.

author: Richard Bauckham
source_title: Jesus and the God of Israel
interpretation: Bauckham reads the statement within the framework of Jewish monotheistic identity. Jesus is not claiming to be a second god but to be included in the unique identity of Israel's one God. The Jewish audience's reaction — picking up stones for blasphemy — confirms they understood the claim correctly: Jesus was placing himself within the divine identity, which to non-believers constituted blasphemy.
context: Bauckham's 'divine identity' Christology argues that the earliest Christian claims about Jesus were not a departure from monotheism but a redefinition of who the one God is — a move already visible in John's Gospel.
